content: Very interesting challenge that would be super fun to code!  I am just concerned that the true count part may be too complex to understand in the time limit if you dont already have a basic understanding of card counting. I appreciate that there is an explanation provided which is helpful but the explanation is very long so it may take too long to understand it and still code the solution.

Goal
* Consider formatting 6 (in "6 decks") and 52 as a {{constants}}. Especially 6 because that's different from most card game contributions which assume using a single deck.

* For clarity, consider converting the value assignment explanation to a three-bullet point list, one bullet point for each value, or to a monospace-format table.

* "add up the numbers": Consider changing "numbers" to "values" for consistency and clarity.

* Example
  • Consider changing 10 to T for clarity.

  • 7-9 appeared 17 times, not 6 times (and make corresponding adjustments to the rest of the example too; 27 + 6 + 36 ≠ 79 in the first place anyway 🤭).

Input
* Please change 10 to T.

Constraints
* Please add the lower and upper bounds for the length of [[cards]] as a constraint. (The upper bound should be less than 52 * 6.)

Thanks.
All suggestions have been implemented. Thank you for your feedback as always! 😊

Thank you for your feedback. 

I appreciate your comment on the "true count" part of the problem. The reason I include the true count in the problem is to make the problem a little bit harder. 

If you have any suggestions for making my explanation less wordy, that would be great! 

Thank you!

Glad to help!

Goal
* In the example, considering changing 10 to T, since 10 doesn't appear anywhere else in the statement.

Thanks.
